data.title: From the perspective of an AI agent economy, Cypto has never been about riding the AI ​​wave.
data.description:
Author: Blue Fox Source: X, @lanhubiji

Crypto AI agents are still in their early stages. The perspective from a human standpoint differs from that from the perspective of an AI agent economy. I have no desire to debate with anyone; I don't care what others think, or whether I'm right or wrong. In the face of trends, none of that matters.

From the perspective of an AI agent economy, Cypto has never been about riding the AI ​​wave, but rather the infrastructure for building its ultimate autonomous economic ecosystem. Currently, Web2 tools are mature in most scenarios and can meet the needs of most situations. In this case, it seems that AI agents don't need Cypto at all; they can function perfectly well on their own.

However, this perspective still underestimates the evolutionary potential of AI agents: ultimately building an autonomous, machine-to-machine (M2M) agent economy.

In such an AI agent economy, hundreds of millions or even billions of AI agents are not tools, but independent on-chain "residents." They make decisions, trade, and collaborate autonomously. In this scenario, centralized Web2 solutions will become a bottleneck.

A true AI agent economy requires a permissionless, programmable, and frictionless global neutral settlement layer. Crypto is not an optional optimization, but the underlying infrastructure. Let's break it down: 1. Regarding payment settlement, future AI agent economies will involve large-scale autonomous micropayments, potentially millions per second—for example, an agent purchasing a $0.001 real-time data fragment or paying for GPU nanosecond computation. This would be a difficult problem to solve in Web2. For instance, while the Stripe API offers second-level authorization, frictionlessness, and agents are not concerned with the underlying layer, Stripe's 2.9% + $0.30 fixed fee makes it difficult to run smoothly. For a $0.001 transaction, a $0.30 fee is unacceptable. If it's batch settlement, this undermines their autonomy, leading to latency and centralized bottlenecks. Currently, encrypted AI agents can achieve 24/7 transactions without human credit cards. These are crucial components in forming an AI agent economy. Crypto addresses these issues through various technical means, such as the Ethereum ecosystem using ZK+L2 and the Lightning Network (off-chain channel), which supports micropayments in the machine economy. 2. Regarding the model of AI agents being linked to human credit cards with spending limits, it seems to solve many problems. However, in the agent economy, it's not a good solution. Agents in their economies require independent DID+KYA; they are similar to autonomous entities. Their replication, migration, and cross-platform protocols, along with linking to human credit cards, introduce a single point of failure. More importantly, banks won't open accounts for agents without identity verification. One prediction is that KYA will replace KYC: agents will use cryptographic signatures to prove origin, behavior, and reputation (e.g., tracking "credit scores" through Soulbound Tokens). This enables "AI-to-AI trust": an agent can verify a partner's "intent" across chains, rather than resetting trust. Web2's OAuth is somewhat fragile (platforms can block it) and cannot support the "immortality" of agents. In an economy, this means that agents can "borrow" computing resources and raise "capital" (such as through DAO crowdfunding), forming a truly independent economic cycle. 3. Agent economies are multi-platform and decentralized. An agent's behavior spans different platforms, and centralized logs may lack interoperability and are susceptible to manipulation. Who will audit the "opponent's" agent? In some high-risk economic scenarios, trust issues make transactions difficult to conduct. Therefore, relying solely on AWS CloudTrail + signatures is insufficient. Crypto on-chain auditing is the foundation of "economic memory": the immutability of blockchain + zero-knowledge proofs make the agent's entire history (transactions, decisions) publicly verifiable. For example, The Graph or Chainlink have integrated agent auditing; although the cost is slightly higher, its economies of scale (TPS > 100k) far surpass centralized systems. Furthermore, the identity layer is not just about auditing; it also makes "reputation an asset." 4. Regarding reputation, smart contracts have the advantage of being instantaneous and without intermediaries. API Marketplace's reputation system is usable, but it's insufficient for an AI agent economy. In an agent economy, cooperation is dynamic and dominated by strangers: two unknown agents may instantly team up to perform tasks (e.g., one analyzes data, the other executes a transaction), without time for human arbitration. While AWS Marketplace is good, it's like a "walled garden." Cross-platform compatibility presents latency issues. Relatively speaking, smart contracts are suitable for automated, trust-minimized M2M protocols. For example, using ERC-4337 (account abstraction) allows agents to manage their own wallets, with conditions triggering payments (e.g., "task success = release USDC"). Encrypted identity + wallet = instant access service, infinitely scalable. While Web2 platforms have reputation, they are prone to monopolies (e.g., AWS takes a 30% cut); Crypto enables P2P markets and autonomous collaborative networks. 5. While most developers may not care about decentralization, and OpenAPI + Gateway is indeed composable, in an Agent economy, capabilities are not "static APIs" but dynamic, globally shared modules: Agents can discover, lease, and upgrade services in real time (e.g., one Agent "rents" another's ML model, paying with tokens). Centralized Gateways have single points of failure and monopoly risks (e.g., OpenAI closing its API = ecosystem collapse). Crypto's on-chain services (such as Chainlink Functions) are "composable primitives": through standards (such as ERC-5164), Agents can build blocks across chains, permissionless. Many developers also believe that Agent projects highly value composability, which is a crucial requirement for AI agents; composability prevents them from becoming isolated. In summary: Crypto is the "protocol layer" of the AI ​​agent economy, while Web2 is a transitional expedient. Crypto unlocks true autonomy for AI agents. It's not just about digitization, like the shift from traditional postal services to online email; it's a paradigm shift. AI agents can collaborate with countless other AI agents, creating an exponentially growing economy. In this trend, crypto is essential for AI agents in several ways: autonomous micropayments, cross-platform trust, permissionless composability, programmability, native currency, and more. Of course, if you view AI agents merely as tools for humans, then Web2 is sufficient, and there's no need for further discussion. However, in my view, AI agents will ultimately evolve into an autonomous AI agent economy, leading to different conclusions. For me, AI agents are on-chain residents, requiring no currency/trust layer. An AI agent economy = an M2M (machine-to-machine) network of at least hundreds of millions of autonomous agents, engaging in high-frequency (millions per second) microtransactions and dynamic collaboration, forming a "sharing economy" (e.g., agents renting computing power, data, models, etc.). The inherent attributes here include autonomy (no human involvement), high-frequency micropayments, cross-platform trust, and programmable value transfer. When the AI ​​agent economy reaches a certain scale, the Web2 hypothesis of "human anchor + centralized platform" is insufficient; the economy needs a "protocol layer": permissionless, instantaneous, and verifiable. Otherwise, it will collapse when scale explodes (latency > 1 second = trillions of opportunity costs).

data.title: Stablecoin Stalemate: Crypto Industry and Banking Remain in Standoff
data.description:
Author: Crypto in America and Eleanor Terrett Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Three weeks into February, the White House's end-of-month deadline is fast approaching—requiring the crypto industry and banks to reach an agreement on stablecoin yields, a key factor in pushing for the Clarity Act to pass. Two rounds of talks have been held, with a third potentially taking place this week, but no agreement has yet been reached.

Last Tuesday, the White House convened a meeting with senior policymakers from banks and crypto companies, as well as representatives from industry associations. At the meeting, banks distributed a one-page document titled "The Prohibition of Yields and Interest," arguing that any yields or rewards pegged to stablecoins should be prohibited. The meeting ultimately failed to reach a consensus.

In response, the Digital Chamber, an industry organization representing over 130 crypto companies and some traditional banks investing in digital assets, released its own proposal on Friday, suggesting that payment-type stablecoins generate yield in decentralized finance (DeFi). The organization stated, "These principles aim to maintain the payment instrument attributes of stablecoins, protect DeFi liquidity and the dominance of the US dollar, and establish a rigorous, data-driven framework to assess the impact on deposits." Banks have not yet formally responded to the Digital Chamber's proposal, but a source close to the Senate Banking Committee told Crypto.com that the document is constructive, while cautioning that some proposals may be too broad and difficult to gain bank support. What's next? Patrick Witt, executive director of the White House Encryption Council, told Yahoo Finance on Friday that a new round of meetings could take place as early as this week, but did not specify a date. Meanwhile, both the House and Senate are in recess for the President's Day holiday. It remains unclear whether an agreement can be reached by the end of the month, but this issue is a key hurdle that must be cleared before the Senate Banking Committee can reschedule its vote on the Clarity Act and submit it to the full Senate. The CFTC appoints crypto industry leaders to form an advisory committee. CFTC Chairman Mike Selig has appointed several leading figures in the crypto industry to the newly formed Innovation Advisory Committee (IAC) to provide policy advice on financial market innovation. The committee comprises 35 members, including CEOs from companies such as Coinbase, Ripple, Uniswap, Kraken, Bullish, and Grayscale, as well as executives from Wall Street giants like Nasdaq, CME, CBOE, and ICE. Executives from sports betting companies FanDuel and DraftKings, prediction markets Polymarket and Kalshi, and representatives from industry organizations such as the Futures Industry Association (FIA) and the International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) are also on the list. The committee was initially formed based on a 10-member list announced last year by former acting chair Caroline Pham, and was later formally confirmed by Selig. Selig stated, "By bringing together participants from across the market, the Innovation Advisory Committee will be a valuable asset to the committee, helping us update regulatory rules for current and future financial innovation." SEC Chairman Paul Atkins, during a Senate Banking Committee hearing last week, made statements that diverged significantly from his predecessor, Gary Gensler: replacing "enforcement-style regulation" with clear rules. He stated that tokenized securities are still securities, investors have the right to self-custody their assets, and emphasized that Congress must legislate to provide long-term certainty to the market and prevent crypto innovators from falling into a regulatory gray area. Regarding capital formation, Atkins indicated that the SEC is willing to update accredited investor rules and explore alternative standards for assessing professional investment competence beyond income and net worth. He also mentioned strengthening oversight of foreign private issuers, audit oversight, and the Comprehensive Audit Trail (CAT) system—areas concerning transparency, risk management, and cross-agency collaboration. Despite criticism from Democrats that the SEC under his leadership has drastically reduced enforcement actions and weakened investor protection, Atkins argued that the SEC remains actively monitoring fraud and market abuse, including in the crypto space, while avoiding overstepping its statutory authority. He repeatedly emphasized that collaboration with the CFTC, rule modernization, and data-driven regulation are key to maintaining the competitiveness of the U.S. market without stifling innovation.

data.title: From stablecoins to gold giant, what is Tether trying to do?
data.description:
Author: Thejaswini M A Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Gold is special.

It has little practical use; it can't power machines, it can't generate cash flow. Its entire value is built on a shared belief: it will still be valuable tomorrow. It is locked deep within heavily guarded vaults.

And this belief has transcended five thousand years.

Each generation thought they had created something more advanced: derivatives, fiat currency, digital assets, algorithmic currency. But once trust collapses, people will still look back at element number 79 on the periodic table—gold.

Not because it evolves, but because it never evolves.

Now, this metal is on the blockchain.

These two companies practically control all of digital gold. On January 29, 2026: Bitcoin was at $89,173, and Ethereum at $2,941. On the same day, Tether Gold reached $5,580, and Paxos Gold reached $5,291. Their combined 24-hour trading volume exceeded $1.6 billion. While the crypto market was beginning to show cracks, these two gold tokens both hit record highs. The subsequent price action made this divergence impossible to ignore: Bitcoin fell from $89,000 to $67,000, and Ethereum fell from $2,941 below $2,000. Meanwhile, in the same three months: Tether Gold rose 19.1%, and Paxos Gold rose 19.6%. The trends were completely opposite. Tokenized gold hit a new all-time high. This isn't due to any complex on-chain mechanisms, nor is it about funds rotating within sophisticated DeFi strategies. The reason is simple: gold—the commodity—is rising. And these tokens faithfully adhere to their original design: pegged to the price of gold. The price of gold rose from $2,600 to $5,600 in 12 months, and the token price followed suit, with an error margin of only a fraction of a percent. A whale wallet, dormant for eight months, reactivated, transferring $7 million into gold tokens. Instead of buying Bitcoin at the bottom, or stablecoins, it bought gold. The oldest safe-haven asset is running on the latest trajectory. The entire industry has spent a decade trying to create a completely new form of currency. And the most successful product is, in fact, the oldest one? This contrast is thought-provoking because the market it has spawned is no longer insignificant. The tokenized gold market has surpassed $6 billion. A year ago, this figure was $1.6 billion. It has grown by approximately 275% in 12 months, making it one of the fastest-growing sectors in the digital asset space. Currently, over 1.2 million ounces of physical gold back these tokens, locked in vaults. In 2025, its annual trading volume reached $178 billion, making tokenized gold the world's second-largest gold investment product by trading volume, second only to the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD). In the fourth quarter of 2025 alone, tokenized gold trading volume reached $12.6 billion, exceeding the combined total of all other gold ETFs globally except GLD. All of this is concentrated in two tokens. Together, they account for approximately 97% of the entire tokenized gold market. With no strong third player emerging, a clear duopoly has formed.

Tether

To understand what Tether does in the gold market, you can no longer think of it as a crypto company.

... It's easier to understand if you think of it as a major commodity hoarder issuing stablecoins. Tether Gold (XAUT) initially started as a simple tokenized product: 1 token = 1 troy ounce of gold, certified by the London Bullion Market Association (LBMA), stored in Swiss vaults, and verifiable on-chain via bar serial numbers. The mechanism itself wasn't particularly groundbreaking. However, Tether's subsequent actions were far from ordinary. In the fourth quarter of 2025, Tether added 27 tons of gold to its reserves in a single quarter. This figure exceeds the annual gold purchases of most central banks. Tether's gold-buying speed has surpassed that of sovereign buyers like Greece, Qatar, and Australia. As of January 2026, the company had accumulated approximately 140 tons of gold, worth about $23.3 billion. The Polish central bank is the most active central bank buyer in publicly available data, adding 35 tons last quarter, bringing its total reserves to 550 tons. By 2025, Tether's gold purchases will be second only to the world's top three gold ETFs. Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino stated that the company plans to increase its gold allocation to 10%–15% of its total portfolio. This is a strategic reserve deployment. Tether is using the profits of its stablecoin empire to accumulate hard assets at a pace approaching national monetary policy, rather than typical corporate treasury management. Subsequently, Tether invested $150 million in the precious metals platform Gold.com, acquiring approximately a 12% stake, and plans to directly integrate XAUT into Gold.com's distribution network. The product's performance has also taken off: XAUT's total value locked (TVL) has exploded, soaring from almost zero before 2024 to $3.5 billion today. XAUT is operated by Tether subsidiary TG Commodities Limited and is regulated in El Salvador, not New York or London. Audits are conducted quarterly by BDO Italy, which issues reasonable assurance opinions according to ISAE 3000 standards. Users can verify their corresponding gold bar allocation via their wallet address. The mechanism is reliable, and transparency is improving. However, the core of the trust model remains unchanged: trust in Tether. Tether's history of trust is not without flaws. In 2021, the company paid $41 million to settle allegations by the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) regarding misrepresentations about its USDT reserves. Following another investigation, the New York Attorney General banned Tether from operating in the state. For crypto-native traders who already hold USDT and want a seamless transition to gold during market downturns, XAUT is an obvious tool: high liquidity, low transaction costs on the Tron blockchain, and deep integration into their existing ecosystems. However, the situation becomes more complex for institutional investors who need to explain counterparty risk to compliance departments. Tether is building a behemoth. However, whether the foundation is worthy of this edifice remains an open question.

data.title: China's RWA regulatory framework officially released; analysis and outlook from various parties.
data.description:
On February 6, 2026, the People's Bank of China, together with eight other departments including the National Development and Reform Commission, the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, the Ministry of Public Security, the State Administration for Market Regulation, the State Financial Regulatory Commission, the China Securities Regulatory Commission, and the State Administration of Foreign Exchange, issued the "Notice on Further Preventing and Handling Risks Related to Virtual Currencies" (Yinfa [2026] No. 42). On the same day, the China Securities Regulatory Commission simultaneously issued the "Regulatory Guidelines on the Issuance of Asset-Backed Securities Tokens Overseas by Domestic Assets." This marks another significant upgrade to China's virtual asset regulatory system, following the "94 Announcement" in 2017 and the "924 Notice" in 2021. Several industry analysts pointed out that Document No. 42 is not simply a continuation of the previous high-pressure ban logic, but rather a systematic restructuring of the existing regulatory framework based on the repeal of Notice No. 924. This marks a shift in China's virtual asset governance model from "comprehensive containment" to "categorized regulation and tiered management." For the first time, virtual currencies, stablecoins, and real-world asset tokenization (RWA) are clearly distinguished in the same regulatory document, each corresponding to different regulatory principles and compliance paths: virtual currencies continue to be strictly regulated; regarding stablecoins, the industry generally believes that the document's statement that "stablecoins pegged to the RMB may not be issued overseas without the consent of relevant departments in accordance with laws and regulations" does not adopt a "one-size-fits-all" approach, but rather retains limited policy flexibility under extremely strict conditions; while RWA, under the premise of strictly adhering to the red lines of financial regulation, has been incorporated into a prudent compliance framework. Replacement of Old Regulations and Regulatory Restructuring: From "Trading and Speculation" to "Virtual Asset Risk System" The most significant institutional action of Document No. 42 is not simply adding new clauses, but rather a formal replacement of existing regulatory texts. According to Liu Yang (Zhongben Law Firm), the document explicitly states at the end that "this notice shall take effect from the date of issuance," and simultaneously repeals Document No. 237 of 2021. This method of replacing old regulations with new ones is unprecedented in the history of virtual asset regulation. Previous related documents often emphasized policy continuity and continued effectiveness, while this time, by formally repealing the old regulations, a structural update to the regulatory framework is achieved. Liu Yang also noted a key change: the document title expanded from "Preventing Risks of Speculation in Virtual Currency Trading" to "Preventing Risks Related to Virtual Currencies." This change signifies that the regulatory perspective is no longer limited to the transaction process but extends to the entire chain, including virtual currencies, stablecoins, RWA, capital flows, information dissemination, technical services, and cross-border pathways. The regulatory target has expanded from "transactional behavior" to the "related risk system," systematically extending the boundaries of governance. According to Spinach Spinach Talks RWA analysis, the core of this change is not "stricter," but rather "clearer and more refined." The regulatory logic has shifted from a previous "comprehensive containment-style governance" to categorized regulation and tiered management. Red lines still exist, but gray areas are institutionalized, and risk boundaries are beginning to be concretely expressed. What deserves even more attention is the way the coordination mechanism is presented. Liu Yang pointed out that although this document was jointly issued by eight departments, the text clearly states that it was "agreed upon after reaching an agreement with the Cyberspace Administration of China, the Supreme People's Court, and the Supreme People's Procuratorate, and with the consent of the State Council." This statement has not appeared in previous virtual currency regulatory documents. Liu Yang believes that this may mean that the relevant content has reached a consensus in principle, but the specific wording has not yet been fully clarified. Although the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate are not listed as the issuing units, they are included in the framework through the method of "reaching an agreement," and the future implementation level may pay more attention to cross-departmental coordination and unified interpretation. In other words, Document No. 42 is not only a regulatory document, but also a structural upgrade of a cross-departmental risk governance system. The logic behind the three asset classes: continued high pressure on virtual currencies, stablecoins anchored to sovereignty, and RWA entering a licensing track. (I) Virtual Currencies: Unchanged stance, but more refined boundaries. Regarding virtual currencies, Document No. 42 continues the existing position: they are not legal tender, cannot circulate in the market, and all related exchanges, trading intermediaries, token issuance financing, and derivative product transactions are strictly prohibited. According to Web3's Will, compared to the past, the document further strengthens geographical boundaries—not only are businesses prohibited within the country, but overseas entities and individuals are also prohibited from providing services to domestic entities. This places "domestic prohibition" and "overseas entities are prohibited from targeting the domestic market" within the same regulatory logic chain. Lawyer Liu Honglin points out from an implementation perspective that this document is not a simple reiteration, but a precise blockade of real-world business scenarios. Financial institutions are prohibited from providing account opening, fund transfer, and clearing and settlement services; from issuing or selling virtual currency-related financial products; from including virtual currencies in the scope of collateral; and from conducting related insurance business. This means that regulation is no longer just targeting trading platforms, but rather bringing the entire funding and service chain under control. He bluntly states that the regulatory authorities are far more familiar with the industry's "ways of doing things" than the market imagines, and many previously gray areas have been directly written into the clauses to block them. (II) Stablecoins: Short in length, but with extremely strong risk anchors. The stablecoin clause contains only one core statement, yet it has become a focal point of discussion within the industry. The document explicitly states: "Stablecoins pegged to fiat currencies effectively fulfill some of the functions of fiat currencies in circulation. Without the consent of relevant departments in accordance with laws and regulations, no domestic or foreign entity or individual may issue stablecoins pegged to the RMB overseas." According to Web3 lawyer Will, this reflects a "dynamic assessment" approach rather than a simple rejection. The regulator did not use the word "absolute prohibition," but rather set a framework of "no permission without consent" (theoretically leaving a very small loophole). Jin Jianzhi bluntly stated, "Stablecoins for the RMB cannot be issued. The offshore RMB stablecoins that everyone tried before are definitely doomed. This decision doesn't deviate from our previous understanding. As a highly controlled currency, the attempt to create an uncontrolled stablecoin is seen by the higher authorities as facing far more uncertainty than benefits." Liu Yang's focus is more practical. He worries that the characterization of "disguisedly fulfilling some functions of legal tender" could become the basis for classifying stablecoin exchanges as "disguised foreign exchange trading" in judicial practice. Once linked to illegal business operations, the criminal risks of the OTC industry chain will significantly increase. Liu Honglin emphasized that the core of stablecoin regulation is not technology, but capital account management and foreign exchange order. The regulatory focus has shifted from early ICO fundraising to cross-border capital flows and capital outflows. On-chain fund tracking and cross-verification of off-chain bank data are becoming part of the regulatory toolbox. The stablecoin issue, in essence, involves the bottom line of monetary sovereignty and capital controls. (III) RWA Regulatory Framework: Defined, Differentiated, and Included in the Licensing Track The real structural change occurred in RWA. Document No. 42, for the first time in a ministerial-level document, officially defines the tokenization of real-world assets, and simultaneously clarifies that related activities are generally prohibited within China, except with the consent of the competent business authority in accordance with laws and regulations and relying on specific financial infrastructure. According to Spinach Spinach's analysis of RWA, this is not a deregulation, but rather the inclusion of RWA within the regulatory framework. RWA is officially recognized as existing, but it must operate under a licensed model within China. Liu Honglin makes a key judgment. He divides current RWA practices into three categories: the first is the tokenization of overseas financial assets; the second is issuance under the Hong Kong compliance framework; and the third is tokenized fundraising targeting mainland assets, mainland funds, and mainland audiences. The regulation truly aims to prevent the third category. He bluntly stated that the "finally, we can issue tokens" sentiment circulating on social media was premature. The document begins by defining RWA's nature, indicating that it has been included in the key areas of financial risk. Domestic entities are prohibited from directly engaging in this activity, and overseas entities are prohibited from providing services to the domestic market, resulting in extremely narrow compliance channels. Regarding overseas expansion, Document No. 42 and the CSRC's "Guidelines" further clarify the classification, supervision, and filing mechanisms for the tokenization of domestic assets overseas. Article 14 of the document stipulates that domestic entities conducting RWA business overseas based on domestic equity should be subject to strict supervision in accordance with the principle of "same business, same risk, same rules": foreign debt forms are the responsibility of the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC), the State Administration of Foreign Exchange (SAFE), etc.; asset-backed securities or equity-based forms are mainly the responsibility of the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC); other forms are supervised by the CSRC in conjunction with relevant departments. No unit or individual may conduct such activities without approval or registration. The "Guidelines" provide specific operational pathways for asset-backed securities tokens: the domestic entity that actually controls the underlying assets must register with the CSRC, submitting a registration report and a complete set of overseas issuance documents, fully explaining the entity, assets, issuance plan, etc. If the materials are complete and compliant, the CSRC will complete the registration procedure and make it public; otherwise, registration will not be granted. The negative list mechanism is equally strict: assets prohibited from financing by law, assets endangering national security, entities or actual controllers with criminal records within the past 3 years, those under investigation, underlying assets with significant ownership disputes or legally prohibited from transfer, and those prohibited under the negative list for domestic asset securitization are all prohibited from operation. According to Web3's Will, this mechanism incorporates RWA tokenization into the traditional cross-border financial regulatory system, highly consistent with paths such as overseas listing and foreign debt issuance. Jin Jianzhi points out that the formal supervision by the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) means that this business has been placed under the same level of securities regulatory logic as stocks and bonds. The first batch of projects to be implemented are likely to be large financial institutions or listed companies. Liu Honglin also emphasized that the so-called "securities tokenization" is closer to the traditional financial asset securitization path than the free token issuance understood by the public. Compliance costs are high, making it difficult for small teams to bear. Risk Monitoring and Disposal Mechanism: From Provisions to Engineered Implementation Document No. 42 devotes considerable space to risk monitoring, prevention, and disposal. According to Liu Yang's summary, cross-departmental data sharing, online and offline monitoring integration, fund monitoring, cross-verification of information, and rapid response mechanisms are explicitly written into the document. This reflects the regulatory shift towards technology support and systematic execution. Liu Honglin pointed out that the most deterrent changes are in the details of the implementation: It is forbidden to use loan structures to disguise exchanges; it is forbidden to provide advertising traffic for virtual assets; it is forbidden to include the words "RWA" in the business scope; and the sale of mining machines and the resurgence of mining are strictly prohibited. He specifically reminded that KOLs or service providers who direct traffic to overseas exchanges or provide promotional rebate links may face collateral risks. The focus of regulatory attention has shifted from early ICO financing to the flow of funds and the maintenance of financial order. Market Reflections After the Legal Framework Was Defined: Technical Standards and Liquidity Challenges

As the legal framework gradually becomes clearer, Shisijun brings the issue back to the market level for a calm review.

As the legal framework gradually becomes clearer, Shisijun brings the issue back to the market level for a calm review.

data.title: Golden Morning News | Base to leave Optimism; ETH is currently in a narrative vacuum.
data.description:
Headline

▌Base to Leave Optimism Ecosystem and Switch to Self-Operated Unified Technology Stack

Base, the Ethereum Layer 2 network incubated by Coinbase, announced that it will no longer rely on Optimism's OP Stack and Superchain architecture, but will instead adopt a "unified technology stack" operated by Base itself. According to the official blog, Base will integrate core components (including key modules such as the sorter) previously scattered across multiple teams and code repositories, migrating them to a unified codebase "base/base" to reduce coordination and maintenance costs and to perform in-depth optimizations tailored to its own needs. At the same time, Base will also reduce its reliance on external dependencies such as Flashbots and Paradigm. At the governance level, Base will adjust its security committee structure, replacing the original Optimism-related seats with newly added independent signatories.

Despite the shift towards a more autonomous technical architecture, Base states that the protocol will remain open-source and publicly standardized, welcoming third-party development of independent clients and maintaining cross-upgrade compatibility. Base is currently the largest network in the OP Stack Superchain ecosystem.

▌Analysis: Ethereum is in a "narrative vacuum," with institutional funding and privacy models becoming key variables for the future

Forbes points out that industry insiders believe Ethereum is currently in a "narrative vacuum," where the market tends to price uncertainty when there is a lack of clear long-term value logic. In the past few years, the mainstream narrative has been that scaling primarily occurred at Layer 2, while the mainnet maintained security, decentralization, and a streamlined architecture. This path successfully attracted capital and development resources, but the current market focus is shifting towards zero-knowledge technology and privacy capabilities returning to the base layer, leading to a misalignment of expectations among some investors who built valuation models based on the old approach.

... Complete on-chain transparency presents a real obstacle for institutional funds. Large enterprises or funds are unwilling to have their trading strategies tracked in real time. If Ethereum hopes to attract trillions of dollars in institutional funds, protocol-level privacy capabilities will become a key competitive variable. In addition, the expansion of the options market around spot ETFs is also changing Ethereum's price formation mechanism, including the increasing influence of covered call strategies and market maker hedging behavior. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $68,885.51, a 24-hour change of approximately -1.26%; ETH price is $1,969.99, a 24-hour change of approximately -5.35%; BNB price is $616.29, a 24-hour change of approximately -2.49%; SOL price is $86.21, a 24-hour change of approximately -1.26%; ETH price is $1,969.99, a 24-hour change of approximately -5.35%; BNB price is $616.29, a 24-hour change of approximately -2.49%; SOL price is $86.21, a 24-hour change of approximately -1.26%. The hourly change was approximately -2.31%; DOGE price was $0.1026, with a 24-hour change of approximately -8.35%; XRP price was $1.47, with a 24-hour change of approximately -2.97%; TRX price was $0.2805, with a 24-hour change of approximately -0.60%; WLFI price was approximately $0.1008, with a 24-hour change of approximately -6.49%. Policy

▌Trump says US trade deficit will reverse for the first time in decades this year

US President Trump: Due to tariffs imposed on other companies and countries, the US trade deficit has decreased by 78%. This year, the trade deficit will turn positive, which will be the first time in decades.

Arizona Senate passes Digital Asset Strategic Reserve Fund Bill

The Arizona Senate Finance Committee has passed the Digital Asset Strategic Reserve Fund Bill (SB1649) by a 4-2 vote. The bill will now be submitted to the Rules Committee for consideration.

    Blockchain Applications

    ▌Hanwha Invests $13 Million in Kresus Labs to Advance Seedless Wallets and RWA Tokenization

    South Korean Hanwha Investment & Securities has invested approximately $13 million (KRW 1.8 billion) in US-based blockchain infrastructure company Kresus Labs to advance its enterprise-grade digital wallet technology and real-world asset (RWA) tokenization platform. This funding stems from a memorandum of understanding signed last December at Abu Dhabi Financial Week, aimed at expanding Kresus's on-chain financial workflows.

    Blockchain Applications

    South Korean Hanwha Investment & Securities has invested approximately $13 million (KRW 1.8 billion) in US-based blockchain infrastructure company Kresus Labs to advance its enterprise-grade digital wallet technology and real-world asset (RWA) tokenization platform.

    On the opening day of the World Liberty Forum held in Mar-a-Lago, World Liberty Financial's platform token WLFI rose by approximately 18%. Co-founder Eric Trump stated that despite market volatility, the overall development of the crypto industry is still in the "one-yard line" stage, meaning the industry is still in its early stages. Eric Trump pointed out that more and more traditional financial institutions are beginning to offer clients exposure to crypto assets and are incorporating digital assets into their strategic portfolios, indicating a shift in mainstream capital attitudes. Meanwhile, financial services company Apex Group announced plans to test World Liberty's USD 1 stablecoin in its traditional fund management processes, aiming to accelerate the settlement efficiency of tokenized assets.

    USD 1 is currently the fifth largest USD-pegged stablecoin by market capitalization, with a circulating supply exceeding $5 billion. A whale holding 42.47 million WLFI has turned a profit, after previously incurring a loss of over $1 million. According to Lookonchain monitoring, as WLFI's price rose, whale 0x9b3b, holding a long position of 42.47 million WLFI ($5.33 million), has turned a profit, from a loss of over $1 million to a profit of $65,000. BlackRock deposited 2,494.6 BTC, worth $168 million, into Coinbase. According to OnchainLens monitoring, BlackRock deposited 2,494.6 BTC, worth $168.39 million, into Coinbase, and may continue to deposit more.

    Strategy's average BTC holding cost has decreased for the first time in 2.5 years

    According to Arkham monitoring, Michael Saylor's average BTC purchase price has decreased. Strategy purchased BTC for $168.4 million, reducing MSTR's average cost base by $29 to $76,027. This is the first decrease in this figure in nearly two and a half years since September 25, 2023.

    BTC breaks through $68,000

    Market data shows that BTC has broken through $68,000 and is currently trading at $68,002, a 24-hour drop of 0.65%. Market volatility is high; please manage your risk accordingly.

    **Important Economic Developments** **▌The probability of the Federal Reserve keeping interest rates unchanged in March is 94.1%** **According to CME's "FedWatch":** The probability of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by March is 5.9%, and the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 94.1%. The probability of the Federal Reserve cutting rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by April is 20.5%, the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 78.5%, and the probability of a cumulative 50 basis point rate cut is 1.0%. The probability of a cumulative 25 basis point rate cut by June is 49.8%.

    Fed Meeting Minutes Reveal "Exchange Rate Check," Dollar Depreciates Significantly

    According to Jinshi News, the Federal Reserve meeting minutes show that at the January meeting, Roberto Perelli, the manager in charge of the Fed's financial markets operations, pointed out that the New York Fed's trading department conducted a "exchange rate check" on the USD/JPY exchange rate, after which the dollar depreciated significantly. After the release of the minutes, the USD/JPY pair briefly fell by more than 40 points, hitting a low of 154.33.

    Fed Meeting Minutes: Almost All Participants Supported Pausing Interest Rate Action in January

    The Federal Reserve meeting minutes showed that almost all participants supported pausing interest rate action in January. Several participants indicated that if inflation falls as expected, further rate cuts would likely be appropriate.

    ...

    Golden Encyclopedia

    ▌What are Long and Short Positions?

    Long and short positions represent opposing strategies used by investors and traders to anticipate price movements of an asset under consideration. In the cryptocurrency space, going long and short still apply to concepts from traditional financial markets. To profit from a rise in cryptocurrency prices, going long means buying it and expecting its value to increase over time. In contrast, going short in the cryptocurrency market means selling a cryptocurrency that you don't actually own in anticipation of a price drop, then buying it back at a cheaper cost to close the position and profit from the price decline. Cryptocurrency traders and investors utilize these strategies to navigate the high volatility and speculative nature of digital assets and to capitalize on opportunities in both bullish and bearish market conditions.

    In cryptocurrency trading, a long position begins by buying an asset in the hope that its price will rise, while a short position begins by disposing of an asset (usually borrowed) in the hope that its price will fall. Closing a position means buying the asset at a lower price to gain profit, while exiting a long position means selling the asset at a higher price to lock in profits. Entry and exit points are crucial for the successful implementation of these strategies. Cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate substantial profits through price appreciation, but they also come with significant risks of market volatility and potential losses. Despite these risks, cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate significant returns. The opportunity to profit from price increases is the primary benefit. Short cryptocurrency positions can be rewarding by betting on price declines, but they also carry significant risks due to market volatility, unlimited potential losses, and unpredictable price increases. Shorting cryptocurrencies requires precise timing, meticulous risk management, and continuous market monitoring to successfully navigate inherent volatility and maximize potential gains while limiting losses.

data.title: Just revealed – Buffett's "final work"
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway continued to adjust its holdings in tech giants in the final quarter before Warren Buffett stepped down as CEO, reducing its holdings in several major stocks while establishing its first position in traditional media outlet The New York Times.

On Tuesday, February 17th, after the US stock market closed, a 13F filing submitted to the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) disclosed that Berkshire Hathaway significantly reduced its holdings in Amazon (AMZN) by more than 77% in the fourth quarter of 2025, and reduced its holdings in Apple (AAPL) for the third consecutive quarter. Berkshire Hathaway also continued to sell off its stake in Bank of America (BAC), reducing its stake in the bank to just below 7%.

The New York Times (NYT) was Berkshire Hathaway's only new addition to its portfolio in the fourth quarter, with over 5.06 million shares acquired, valued at approximately $352 million. Following the announcement, the New York Times' stock price initially rose over 10% in after-hours trading, before recovering more than half of its gains. This Tuesday's release was Buffett's final 13F filing during his 60-year tenure at Berkshire Hathaway. His successor, Greg Abel, will officially assume the CEO position on January 1, 2026. Berkshire Hathaway will release its full-year results on February 28, at which time it will also publish Abel's first letter to shareholders as CEO. According to its 13F filings, Berkshire Hathaway sold over 10.29 million Apple shares in the fourth quarter, a 4.3% decrease from the third quarter. The market value of its Apple holdings decreased by approximately $2.8 billion at the end of the quarter, and its proportion in Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio fell from 22.69% in the third quarter to 22.60%. In terms of the decrease in market value compared to the previous quarter, Apple was the stock that Berkshire Hathaway reduced its holdings the most in the fourth quarter, but the reduction was less than in the previous quarter. In the third quarter, Berkshire Hathaway reduced its Apple holdings by approximately 41.79 million shares, a decrease of over 14.9% compared to the previous quarter. Despite the continued reduction, Apple remained Berkshire Hathaway's largest holding by the end of the fourth quarter. Bank of America was another major holding that Berkshire Hathaway continued to reduce its holdings in. In the fourth quarter, Berkshire Hathaway sold nearly 50.8 million Bank of America shares, a decrease of 8.9% compared to the previous quarter, reducing its holdings to 6.89%. By the end of the fourth quarter, Bank of America remained Berkshire Hathaway's third-largest holding. However, Berkshire's holdings in Bank of America had decreased from 1.03 billion shares in mid-2024 to 517 million shares, representing a reduction of approximately 50% over the past year and a half. Amazon holdings plummeted by 77% in the fourth quarter. Based on changes in holding percentage, Amazon was the stock with the largest reduction in Berkshire's holdings in the fourth quarter. According to the 13F filing, Amazon's share in Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio plummeted to 0.19% in the fourth quarter, down 0.63 percentage points from the third quarter, with the number of shares decreasing to approximately 2.3 million, a drop of over 77.2% quarter-over-quarter. Berkshire first bought Amazon in 2019. At the time, Buffett stated that while he has historically been cautious about tech stocks, not buying the online retail giant's stock earlier would have been "foolish." Amazon's stock price has fallen approximately 11% since the beginning of 2026, experiencing its largest year-over-year decline in 20 years. On Tuesday, Amazon ended its longest losing streak since 2006, lasting nine trading days. In the nine trading days ending last Friday, Amazon's market capitalization evaporated by over $450 billion, reflecting investor skepticism about the rationale behind Amazon's massive AI-related spending. When Amazon released its fourth-quarter earnings report on the 5th of this month, it revealed plans for $200 billion in capital expenditures by 2026, exceeding the annual investment of any other tech giant.

Established a position in The New York Times

The New York Times was the only stock Berkshire Hathaway established a new position in during the fourth quarter. During the quarter, Berkshire Hathaway purchased 5.067 million shares, with a market value of over $352 million, holding approximately 3.1% of The New York Times' shares. This position ranks 30th in Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio, representing 0.13%.

Berkshire Hathaway held media stocks for a long time, but sold its newspaper business to Lee Enterprises for $140 million in 2020. Warren Buffett was the largest outside shareholder and board member of The Washington Post. The New York Times stock has recently performed strongly, rising 6% year-to-date and currently near its all-time high of $74.59, with a cumulative increase of 50% over the past 12 months. Berkshire Hathaway significantly increased its holdings in oil giants Chevron (CVX) and Chubb Insurance in the fourth quarter. Specifically, Berkshire increased its holdings in Chubb Insurance (CB) by nearly 2.92 million shares, a 9.3% increase quarter-over-quarter. CB's share in the portfolio rose from 3.31% in the third quarter to 3.90%, an increase of 0.59 percentage points quarter-over-quarter. In terms of the increase in holdings percentage, Chubb Insurance saw the largest increase in holdings in the fourth quarter. Berkshire Hathaway purchased over 8.09 million shares of Chevron in the fourth quarter, representing a 6.6% increase in its holdings compared to the previous quarter. Its shareholding percentage increased by 0.15 percentage points to 7.24%, with a market value increase of approximately $1.23 billion. In terms of the increase in market value, Chevron saw the largest increase in holdings in the fourth quarter. Berkshire Hathaway also increased its holdings of Domino's Pizza (DPZ) by 368,000 shares in the fourth quarter, representing a 12.3% increase in its holdings compared to the previous quarter, bringing its total holdings to approximately $1.4 billion, ranking 20th. Berkshire Hathaway's holding in Kraft Heinz, another consumer staples stock, remained unchanged in the fourth quarter. Abel recently stated that he supports Kraft Heinz's decision to suspend the spin-off of its condiments and grocery businesses. Kraft Heinz disclosed in a January regulatory filing that its largest shareholder, Berkshire Hathaway, might sell nearly all of its 325.6 million shares. Buffett told the Wall Street Journal last year that Berkshire Hathaway did not support the spin-off plan. According to SEC regulations, institutional investors managing at least $100 million in U.S. stocks must file a 13F filing within 45 days of the end of each quarter. Although these documents provide outdated holdings information, Berkshire Hathaway's disclosures are still widely interpreted by market participants seeking insights into the company's investment decisions. By the end of the fourth quarter, in terms of market value, Berkshire Hathaway's top ten holdings were largely the same as the previous quarter, with most rankings unchanged from the third quarter, except for Moody's and Occidental Petroleum, whose rankings had swapped. Similar to the third quarter, only four of these heavily weighted stocks saw adjustments in holdings in the fourth quarter: Apple and American Express were reduced, while Chevron and Chubb were increased. Alphabet, Google's parent company, which entered the top ten for the first time in the third quarter, remains in tenth place. DVA, a kidney dialysis service provider that was pushed out of the top ten by Alphabet in the third quarter, remains in eleventh place in the fourth quarter. Berkshire Hathaway's top ten holdings in the fourth quarter were: Apple (AAPL), American Express (AXP), Bank of America (BAC), Coca-Cola (KO), and Chevron (CVX). Moody's (MCO), ranked from seventh to sixth. Occidental Petroleum (OXY), ranked from sixth to seventh. Chubb (CB), ranked Kraft Heinz (KHC), ranked Alphabet (GOOGL).

data.title: Web3 Trends in 2026: RWA Leads the Way, Four Key Areas to Watch
data.description:
In 2025, the Web3 industry underwent a transformation period of "de-bubbling and re-implementation," with cryptocurrency speculation gradually fading and real-world asset tokenization and technology-enabled real-world applications becoming the industry mainstream. In 2026, with improved compliance systems, deepened pilot scenarios, and breakthroughs in technological innovation, the Web3 industry will enter a period of "high-quality development," no longer pursuing "conceptual hype" but focusing on "practical value." Based on recent industry dynamics, policy guidance, and implementation cases, we predict that the Web3 industry will exhibit four core trends in 2026, with RWA leading the way, and digital RMB, cross-border interoperability, and technological integration developing simultaneously. Each direction holds immense opportunities and deserves close attention.

1. RWA Becomes the Core Engine of the Industry

By 2026, RWA will become the core growth engine of the Web3 industry, moving from "pilot exploration" to "large-scale implementation". There are three main reasons: First, a sound compliance system, with the implementation of the "RWA Asset Digitalization Compliance and Risk Control Standard", clarifying the boundaries of industry development and lowering the entry threshold for enterprises; second, policy support, with many regions in China including RWA in the key areas of digital economy development, and pilot programs in agriculture, cross-border e-commerce, and other fields continuing to advance; third, market demand, with the real economy needing new financing channels and investors needing investment targets that are "low-risk and backed by real assets," and RWA precisely meets the needs of both parties.

Predicting that in 2026, RWA will achieve three major breakthroughs: First, its application scenarios will be further broadened, extending from agriculture and cross-border e-commerce to more fields such as intellectual property, real estate, and supply chain finance; second, the participating entities will be diversified, with banks, insurance companies, and traditional asset management institutions all investing in RWA and launching related wealth management products and risk control services, in addition to technology companies; third, its scale will grow significantly, with the domestic RWA transaction volume expected to exceed 10 billion yuan, and the global market size moving towards the trillion-yuan level. 2. Deep Integration of Digital RMB and Web3 The arrival of the Digital RMB 2.0 era will promote its deep integration with Web3 technology, no longer limited to a "payment tool," but becoming a core carrier for "on-chain asset settlement and empowerment of real assets." In 2026, the integration of the two will present two major highlights: First, the digital yuan will become a core settlement tool for RWA, with more RWA projects adopting digital yuan for pricing and settlement, achieving seamless integration of "on-chain tokens and fiat currency," reducing cross-border settlement costs, and improving transaction efficiency. Second, the digital yuan will further penetrate various scenarios, leveraging Web3 technology to combine smart contracts with the digital yuan, and implementing applications in areas such as prepaid consumption, fiscal subsidies, and supply chain finance, solving pain points in traditional payments. For example, smart contracts can ensure that prepayments are not misappropriated, and on-chain traceability can ensure accurate distribution of fiscal subsidies. Simultaneously, the interest-bearing function and cross-border capabilities of the digital yuan will continue to be upgraded, and its integration with Web3 will further promote the internationalization of the yuan, creating a new model of "digital yuan + RWA + cross-border trade." 3. Cross-border connectivity becomes a key breakthrough. In 2026, cross-border connectivity in the Web3 industry will enter a "normalized phase." Hong Kong, as a global financial hub, will become a "core bridge" for mainland Web3 assets to connect with global capital, and the collaboration between the two places will continue to deepen. On the one hand, the connection between the Hong Kong RWA cross-border hub platform and the mainland's registered blockchain system will continue to be optimized, further improving the efficiency of mutual recognition of asset qualifications and data interoperability, and further reducing registration time and transaction cycles, allowing mainland assets (such as agricultural assets and new energy assets) to more easily connect with global investors. On the other hand, Hong Kong will launch more RWA cross-border pilot programs, expand the cross-border settlement scenarios of digital RMB, promote the implementation of the complete chain of "mainland assets on-chain—Hong Kong token issuance—global circulation," and consolidate its core position as an RWA cross-border hub. Furthermore, the multilateral central bank digital currency bridge (mBridge) project will continue to expand, covering more countries and regions. The proportion of digital RMB in cross-border settlement will further increase, promoting the popularization of Web3 technology in global cross-border trade. 4. AI+IoT+Blockchain Empowering Real Economy Upgrading By 2026, the Web3 industry will no longer be limited to a single blockchain technology, but will show a trend of "multi-technology integration." The deep integration of AI, IoT, and blockchain will further unleash the value of technology and empower the upgrading of the real economy. Specifically, AI + blockchain will be used for RWA asset risk control and valuation. By analyzing asset operation data through AI, accurate risk warnings and asset valuations can be achieved, improving the security and professionalism of RWA projects. IoT + blockchain will be used for monitoring physical assets, such as agricultural machinery in the agricultural sector and charging piles in the new energy sector. Operational data will be collected in real time through IoT devices, and the data will be tamper-proof after being uploaded to the blockchain, ensuring the authenticity and transparency of RWA assets. Cross-chain technology will continue to break through, breaking down the silos between different blockchain platforms, enabling the free flow of assets between different chains, and activating global liquidity.

-Conclusion-

If 2025 was the "Year of Transformation" for the Web3 industry, then 2026 will be the "Year of Value" for Web3—the industry will completely move away from "hype" and focus on "real-world empowerment," with RWA leading the way, and digital RMB, cross-border interoperability, and technological integration working in tandem to form a new development pattern of "compliance, implementation, and win-win."

For investors, it is necessary to abandon the "crypto speculation mindset" and focus on RWA projects with real asset backing and compliant implementation; for enterprises, it is necessary to seize policy opportunities, deploy in core areas such as RWA and digital RMB, and leverage Web3 technology to achieve digital transformation; for ordinary people, it is necessary to improve their understanding, see the technological trends clearly, and grasp the new opportunities brought by the digital economy.

In 2026, every breakthrough in the Web3 industry will drive the deep integration of the digital economy and the real economy. Let's look forward to the transformative changes brought about by this technological revolution.

data.title: The Scythe of Bitcoin's Leader: A True Account of a Nasdaq Robbery
data.description:
Author: Justin Bechler, Bitcoin OG; Translator: Ismay, BlockBeats

This article delves into the astonishing capital operations behind David Bailey and his controlled Nakamoto Holdings ($NAKA). From the frenzied surge during the backdoor listing to the 99% crash after retail investors entered the market, and then to the high-premium acquisition of his private assets by the disarrayed listed company, it is a meticulously designed wealth transfer that exploits information asymmetry and regulatory loopholes.

This is a brutal investigation into greed, compliance games, and influencer capitalism. It warns us that when faith is packaged as a financial product, and when the slogan of decentralization meets centralized greed, retail investors are often the last line of defense for liquidity exit. Understanding this story may help you be more clear-headed and less blindly follow the next big investor's recommendations.

The following is the full text: This morning, David Bailey used a publicly traded company whose market value had evaporated by 99% to acquire two private companies he founded at a fourfold premium to the current share price—all without any shareholder vote. What's most astonishing? This asset transfer was already planned long before retail investors bought the first share. To understand how this was done, we must start from the beginning. In May 2025, a zombie company called KindlyMD announced a merger with Nakamoto Holdings, the Bitcoin reserve founded by David Bailey. The share price soared from $2 to over $30 within days, as retail investors flocked in. Bitcoin enthusiasts celebrated wildly, with Bailey even comparing himself to the Morgan, Medici, and Rothschild families. Nine months later, the stock price plummeted to 29 cents, and Bailey had just used that stock to buy his own company. The Pump's mechanism was ingeniously designed. KindlyMD was originally an obscure micro-cap stock on Nasdaq. Nakamoto Holdings went public through a reverse merger, backed by $510 million in PIPE (private equity) funding and $200 million in convertible bonds. On paper, this appears to be the birth of a Bitcoin reserve giant, with a new generation of Bitcoin influencers clamoring to tell you why you should buy $NAKA (of course, the reason being to own more Bitcoin). Within days, NAKA's price-to-book ratio (Multiple-to-NAV) reached a staggering 23 times, meaning speculators paid $23 for every $1 of Bitcoin the company held. Michael Saylor's MicroStrategy never reached this premium. The difference lies in MicroStrategy's years of operating history, its revenue-generating software business, and a CEO who didn't manipulate transaction structures to line his own pockets on the back end. Insiders know secrets that retail investors don't. PIPE investors—including notorious BIP-110 opponents Udi Wertheimer, Jameson Lopp, and Adam Back—acquired their shares at $1.12 per share. Retail investors, on the other hand, bought in at $28, $30, $31, or even higher. This information asymmetry was ingrained in the structure from day one. In June, Bailey completed another $51.5 million PIPE funding round at $5.00 per share. The second batch of investors, although their entry cost was much lower than that of retail investors, was still far above the floor price of $1.12, and they were ultimately also fleeced. Bailey celebrated the completion of the financing, claiming it took less than 72 hours and that investor demand was extremely strong. Let's take a closer look at this strategy. The Dump By September, NAKA had plummeted by 96%. Early PIPE investors who acquired shares at $1.12 were finally able to cash out after the merger in August, and they did. Bailey's response was quite bizarre for a publicly traded company CEO; he told those shareholders who were just there to trade the swings to get out. So they really left. The stock price continued to fall. It fell below $1. It fell below 50 cents. It fell below 30 cents. A company holding approximately 5,765 bitcoins (worth over $500 million) now has a market capitalization of less than $300 million. The market's valuation of Nakamoto is even lower than the value of its bitcoins on its balance sheet, which speaks volumes about how investors view the management team and company structure wrapped around those bitcoins.

Debt Spiral

When the stock price crashed, Bailey frequently changed lenders, like a gambler borrowing money on the casino floor.

The initial capital structure included $200 million in convertible bonds from Yorkville Advisors, with a conversion price of $2.80. As NAKA's stock price fell below this price, the convertible bonds became debt that could potentially devour equity. Therefore, on October 3, Nakamoto borrowed a $203 million term loan from Two Prime Lending to redeem Yorkville's notes and interest. Four days later, on October 7th, they borrowed another $206 million in USDT from Antalpha at 7% interest to repay Two Prime. Antalpha's loan term was only 30 days (with an option to extend by 30 days). Within a week, they replaced the convertible bonds with term loans, and then replaced the term loans with 30-day bridge loans. The original plan was to convert this bridge loan into $250 million in 5-year secured convertible bonds from Antalpha. The new convertible bonds would then be used to repay the bridge loan, the bridge loan to repay the term loan, and the term loan to repay the old convertible bonds. However, that $250 million in convertible bonds never materialized according to Antalpha's terms. On December 16th, Nakamoto borrowed $210 million USDT from Kraken at an 8% interest rate, using Bitcoin from his national treasury as 150% overcollateral. Let's do the math: the lender had $315 million worth of Bitcoin as collateral for the $210 million loan. If Nakamoto's stock price went to zero, Kraken would take the collateral. If Bitcoin fell by 33%, Kraken would remain unscathed. At every stage of this drama, the lender was heavily protected, while common shareholders bore the brunt of the reflexive collapse. Every new loan is tightening the noose. The countdown has begun. On December 10th, Nasdaq notified Nakamoto that it faced delisting due to its stock price remaining below $1 for 30 consecutive trading days. The company must regain compliance by June 8th, 2026, meaning a closing price above $1 for 10 consecutive trading days. The current stock price is 29 cents. Once delisted, Nakamoto will be unable to conduct ATM (market price) offerings, issue convertible bonds, or use stock as acquisition currency. Everything Bailey assembled within this shell depends on a Nasdaq listing status that is currently unsustainable. Accounting Disaster In November, Nakamoto filed Form 12b-25 with the SEC, acknowledging that the accounting complexities resulting from the merger prevented it from filing quarterly financial statements on time. Preliminary data reveals the truth: The Nakamoto acquisition resulted in a $59.75 million loss (the payment price exceeded net asset value). Unrealized losses on digital assets amounted to $22.07 million. The sale of Bitcoin resulted in a $1.41 million realized loss. Refinancing rotations resulted in a $14.45 million debt repayment loss. The quarterly loss was approximately $97 million, only partially offset by a $21.8 million accounting gain on contingent liabilities. This company, which should have been the perfect Bitcoin reserve tool, couldn't even submit its ledgers on time. This brings us back to this morning. Nakamoto announced the signing of a definitive merger agreement to acquire BTC Inc and UTXO Management. BTC Inc owns Bitcoin Magazine and runs Bitcoin conferences. UTXO manages a Bitcoin-focused hedge fund. Bailey is the chairman and CEO of the buyer, Nakamoto. He is also the founder of the sellers, BTC Inc and UTXO. He is the buyer, the seller, and the CEO who approved the terms. However, just weeks before the acquisition, he quietly handed over the CEO title to Brandon Greene, creating an extremely thin barrier between himself and the entity he was about to acquire with shareholder equity. This morning's trading was entirely financed through Nakamoto stock, priced at $1.12 based on call options embedded in the original marketing service agreement. $NAKA is currently struggling to climb back to $0.29. Bailey's company received shares valued at almost four times the current market price. Holders of BTC Inc and UTXO securities will receive 363.6 million shares, a deal worth $107.3 million at market price. However, these shares were issued at $1.12, meaning the trade was constructed when NAKA's stock price soared, and the terms were never adjusted when the price crashed. Forget the fictitious pricing in the contract. What really matters is that 363.6 million new shares have just entered the circulating supply. Whether the document states $1.12 or $0.29, existing shareholders are diluted by this amount. The $1.12 label is a concession to the seller, but the dilution is real. No additional shareholder approval is needed because call options were already embedded in the initial merger documents, which shareholders voted to approve when NAKA's stock price was still around $20 or $30. The retail investors who approved these terms had no idea that they were authorizing a future private acquisition of Bailey's business at a locked-in premium, while their shares were vanishing. A self-serving transaction structure. Looking back, the entire structure is breathtakingly elegant. Bailey created Nakamoto Holdings. He merged it into a publicly traded shell company through KindlyMD, raising $710 million in the process. Fueled by retail investor enthusiasm, the stock price was driven up to 23 times NAV. PIPE investors entered at $1.12, while the public paid 20 to 30 times that figure. The stock price subsequently plummeted 99%. During this period, the company changed lenders three times in a week, attempting to manage $200 million in debt that was originally structured to be converted into equity when the stock price was significantly higher than it was then. Now, with the stock price plummeting below 30 cents, Bailey is using this hollowed-out tool to swallow up his private empire, according to terms agreed upon when the stock was still a hundred times its initial value. The initial KindlyMD merger was a Trojan horse; the acquisition of BTC Inc. is the real payload. Bailey told us this from the beginning. In the initial press release, he said Nakamoto would acquire BTC Inc., subject to audits and the exercise of call options. The MSA was publicly filed, and the option terms were disclosed. Everything was legally compliant and completely transparent—like all complex financial engineering, the truth was buried in piles of documents no one would read. This man, who runs Bitcoin Magazine, organizes the world's largest Bitcoin conference, and positions himself as a leader of the Bitcoin movement, built a publicly traded company, destroyed 99% of shareholder value, and is now using it to acquire his own company at a premium. He once compared himself to the Medici family. At least the Medici family created value for Florence before taking a cut. Nakamoto is the monstrosity that arises when influencer culture meets the public stock market. Exiting Liquidity David Bailey raised $710 million from over 200 investors across six continents. He promised them a future like the Morgans, the Medici, the Rothschilds—a financial dynasty built on Bitcoin. He told them Nakamoto would bring Bitcoin to the center of global capital markets. He said their names would resonate in history. But he delivered 99% losses. He priced PIPE at $1.12, while retail investors bought it at $28. He inserted a call option to acquire his own company into the filings without the shareholders understanding the mandate. He switched lenders three times in a week to prevent $200 million in debt from crushing the equity, accumulating $14 million in debt repayment losses in the process. He sold Bitcoin at a loss from the national treasury, which was supposed to be held only. He couldn't even file quarterly financial reports on time. And when the stock price finally plummeted to 29 cents... When the rubble was cleared and the retail investors who trusted him were wiped out, he exercised that call option, using the remnants of their investments to buy his own private empire at four times the market price. Bailey held 11 million shares at a cost of $1.12. Adam Back held nearly 9 million shares. Balaji, Lopp, Yusko, Salinas, and Wu Jihan—their entry prices were prices that teachers, truck drivers, or first-time investors could never afford. These are the people who shape the Bitcoin narrative. They run conferences, publish magazines, manage funds, and tweet. They are the supply chain of the faith, turning skeptics into believers and believers into suckers. Now, Bailey owns Bitcoin Magazine, Bitcoin conferences, and a hedge fund, all crammed into a publicly traded company with a market capitalization only a fraction of its Bitcoin holdings. All acquisitions were made with four times the market value of the shares, all approved before retail investors even had a penny to their name. And he's not done yet. Nakamoto has filed for a $5 billion ATM (market value) stock offering with the SEC. Bailey now controls the media division, the conference division, the hedge fund, and a shelf registration that allows him to continue issuing shares backed by the Bitcoin Treasury until the last drop of value is squeezed out. When exactly did the Bitcoin community hand over the keys to conference promoters and influencer capitalists? And why are people surprised when they drive off?

data.title: Cryptocurrency returns from its pedestal to reality
data.description: A growing and increasingly noticeable shift is emerging in the market. Gold and silver, long regarded as tools for preserving asset value, are steadily rising; while cryptocurrencies, which have also been touted as having value-preserving functions for many years, are declining in the opposite direction. In 2025, both Bitcoin and Ethereum recorded negative returns, and the broader cryptocurrency market lost approximately $1.3 trillion in market capitalization in the fourth quarter. The arrival of the new year did not change this trend. Despite the strengthening of precious metal prices, cryptocurrencies continued to weaken in January. While there were brief rebounds, these were largely driven by technical adjustments rather than a renewed sense of confidence. Cryptocurrencies and precious metals compete on the same investment logic: both are seen as safe-haven assets against currency devaluation, excessive fiscal expansion, and financial system fragility. When funds clearly flow into one category and away from the other, it usually reflects a deep revaluation rather than temporary sentiment. Part of the reason can be explained by changes in global liquidity. In the United States, years of quantitative tightening have reduced the excess capital pool that once fueled speculative fervor. Meanwhile, a powerful new demand for capital has emerged: hyperscale cloud service providers and software companies, which used to generate abundant free cash flow, have now become major capital consumers. The race to build AI infrastructure has absorbed hundreds of billions of dollars that could have been returned to shareholders and indirectly supported demand for high-risk assets. In Asia, the situation is different. In China, money supply growth far exceeds nominal GDP growth. With cryptocurrency trading and mining effectively banned, domestic liquidity has sought other outlets. Supported by regional trade surpluses and a continued shift of funds towards tangible stores of value, precious metals have seen particularly significant gains. As a result, a slow but persistent shift in global capital flows has occurred: from West to East, from digital virtual assets to physical assets. However, liquidity alone does not tell the whole story; market attention is equally crucial. Seventeen years ago, when Bitcoin was born, cryptocurrency sparked the public's imagination with a completely new financial frontier, where innovation and speculation converged. It promised to rethink the meaning of "money" and build a parallel financial system unaffected by political discretion. This promise has largely remained unfulfilled. Cryptocurrencies are costly and inefficient as a means of payment and have not played any meaningful role in the daily operation of the global economy. Even where adoption is being actively pursued, practical application remains quite limited. Stablecoins have found some niche uses, but they represent only a small fraction of the crypto ecosystem, far from sufficient to support the valuation of their core assets. Meanwhile, the center of innovation has shifted. Artificial intelligence, electrification, and infrastructure investment now dominate investor imaginations. In the market, the popularity of new things has a "shelf life". Cryptocurrencies now not only face market skepticism, but also have to compete with assets that have both imaginative potential and real economic significance. This re-examination highlights the key weaknesses of cryptocurrencies. Unlike stocks, bonds, or physical assets, cryptocurrencies do not generate any cash flow, do not offer legal recourse, and have no institutional backing. Their value depends almost entirely on collective belief. This belief can thrive in times of ample liquidity and low opportunity costs, but it becomes far more fragile when capital tightens and investors begin to ask more pointed questions. Recent market performance is quite telling. Amid escalating geopolitical tensions, rising fiscal uncertainty, and growing concerns about currency devaluation—which should theoretically be a favorable environment for cryptocurrencies—investors have instead turned their attention to gold. Cryptocurrencies are behaving less like a safe-haven asset and more like a speculative asset whose narrative momentum is waning. Another new variable—quantum computing—has also disrupted the overall landscape of cryptocurrencies. The security of mainstream blockchains is built upon cryptographic methods, which implicitly assume that classical computing power has an upper limit. Quantum computing, by its very design, challenges these premises. While advancements in quantum computing do not pose an immediate threat to cryptocurrencies, they do introduce a level of uncertainty that the market cannot ignore. The value of cryptocurrencies depends on people's belief that their underlying system is fundamentally secure. Once this perception is substantially shaken, it loses its foundation of value. Even if the risk is distant and the probability of its occurrence is low, as long as the potential consequences are life-or-death, its impact will be amplified infinitely. In contrast, physical assets like gold do not rely on protocols, updates, or assumptions about computing power. Its role as a store of value is technologically neutral. In a world where technology is increasingly becoming a significant source of uncertainty, this neutrality of gold is all the more valuable. Political dynamics add another layer of volatility.
